was nice seeing so many devoted Puck readers last night at Doubles for an e=
vent celebrating our C.O.O., Liz Gough. I hadn=E2=80=99t been to the subter=
ranean haunt in the Sherry-Netherland since the 1990s, when I used to occas=
ionally hang out there with my old classmate, the late John F. Kennedy Jr. =

 banking world and catching up with my friend <strong>Meredith Whitney</str=
ong>, the unparalleled research analyst (and loyal Puck

nsition. As my pal <strong>Scott Galloway</strong> has noted, A.I. is not a=
bout to replace bankers <em>per se</em>, but it <em>is</em> going to replac=
e the bankers who aren=E2=80=99t using it.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;

</div>The Twitter journey may have worked out indirectly for Musk. His cann=
ibalized platform, which <strong>Kara Swisher </strong>has dubbed =E2=80=9C=

hing wealth has doubled in recent months, and now sits around $434 billion=
=E2=80=94making the $44 billion Twitter deal seem quaint by comparison. And=

=A0<br/><br/>Not surprisingly, the 2023 founding of xAI, Musk=E2=80=99s art=

=80=94is said to be worth $50 billion already. Since X owns something like =

uyers. We=E2=80=99ll see. Almost any other owner of X would have defaulted =
by now, assuming he or she were relying on the company=E2=80=99s cashflow t=
o make the interest payments. But Musk has been paying the roughly $1.3 bil=
lion in annual interest himself, which is probably why the selling banks th=
ink the paper
should trade at or near par.=C2=A0<br/><br/>Still, that 90 cents strikes me=

uyers that they won=E2=80=99t be able to pick up the X debt on the cheap, i=
t=E2=80=99s a safe bet that the company is no longer worth anything near th=
e $44 billion Elon paid for it. It=E2=80=99s also more than likely that the=
 $31 billion of equity invested in the deal=E2=80=94$24 billion of which ca=
me from Elon and the balance from Elon=E2=80=99s buddies such as <strong>La=
rry
Ellison</strong> and <strong>Marc Andreessen</strong>=E2=80=94is long gone.=
=C2=A0<br/><br/>With the equity at zero, how much is the $13 billion of X d=
ebt worth? Is it 50 cents on the dollar, as I=E2=80=99m sure the distressed=
 debt buyers in the market think, or the 90 to 95 cents that the banks leak=
ing to the <em>Journal </em>are hoping for, with that dash of xAI? We=E2=80=
=99ll soon find out=E2=80=A6 </li></ul></div>

=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Back in August 2019, a young man named <st=
rong>Gabe
Stengel</strong> was visiting his friend <strong>Theo Jacobs</strong> in th=

e the son of my friend <strong>Rick Stengel</strong>, the former <em>Time <=
/em>editor, Snap advisor, and State Department undersecretary. Jacobs is th=
e son of <strong>Ken Jacobs</strong>, then the C.E.O. of Lazard, my former =
firm. Anyway, Gabe was fresh off a quant trading summer internship between =
his junior and senior years at Princeton when he
serendipitously ran into <strong>Peter Orszag</strong>, the former <strong>=
Obama</strong>-era budget director who had joined Lazard in May 2016 as a v=
ice chairman of investment banking. They got to talking.</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">The young Stenge=
l told Orszag that he=E2=80=99d been doing a computer science and quant tra=
ding internship, but he didn=E2=80=99t love it and was fascinated instead b=
y the work his friends were doing in banking. Orszag said he was
contemplating creating a new position at Lazard: a <em>data translator</em>=
 who=E2=80=99d sit between the company=E2=80=99s investment bankers and its=
 data scientists, bridging the gap between the two. =E2=80=9CI thought, tha=
t sounds fascinating,=E2=80=9D Gabe told me recently about his conversation=
 with Orszag that summer afternoon, =E2=80=9Cand I=E2=80=99d love to be abl=
e to use my quantitative skills and learn about business.=E2=80=9D He retur=
ned to Princeton for his senior year, working on his thesis about A.I. assi=
stance for econometrics.</p>

<table class=3D"c4 np" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellsp=

;Inter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0=
px;line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">A.I. hadn=E2=80=99t yet become widely reco=
gnized as the groundshifting
new platform of our time, akin to the internet or mobile technology. Eventu=
ally, after proper interviewing, Stengel got and took that job at Lazard. =
=E2=80=9CI probably worked double the hours for half of the pay,=E2=80=9D h=
e said. =E2=80=9CBut it was great because I got to learn both sides of it=
=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94banking itself, and the tools that bankers needed to do t=
heir jobs more efficiently. He had a lot of ideas about how the firm could =
use A.I., but it was hard to get them implemented. He was fresh out of coll=
ege, after
all.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Over those 19 months or so, he kept return=
ing to the idea of making investment banking more efficient, and giving ban=
kers the tools to do it. =E2=80=9CI spent an inordinate amount of time pull=
ing financials and formatting it in a pretty way and sending it on,=E2=80=
=9D he said, describing one of the main tasks of junior analysts in an inve=
stment bank=E2=80=94the largely thankless yeoman=E2=80=99s work that gobble=
s up huge
amounts of time and leaves armies of young employees sleep deprived and bur=
ned out.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Meanwhile, he started experimenting with=
 GPT-3, which OpenAI had just released. He was convinced there was a better=
 way to do the grunt work of banking. So in 2021, Stengel decided to leave =
Lazard and join up with his Princeton thesis partner, <strong>John Willett<=
/strong>, to start Rogo, an A.I. platform for
financial institutions. (Rogo means <em>I ask </em>in Latin.) =E2=80=9CWhen=
 I was an investment banking analyst at Lazard, I looked up to the firms=E2=
=80=99 extraordinary dealmakers as examples of finance at its best,=E2=80=
=9D Gabe would later write in a post on the Rogo blog. =E2=80=9CI was mesme=
rized by how they effortlessly strategized with <em>Fortune</em> 100 C.E.O.=
s, discussed macro trends, and navigated the complexities of M&A. They =
lived, breathed, and moved markets. If I could have instantly had that insi=
ght and
experience, or have had more time to focus on the fascinating work they wer=
e doing, I might never have left banking. What frustrated me about finance =
was never the long hours=E2=80=93=E2=80=93they were expected=E2=80=93=E2=80=
=93but how hard it was to be as smart and knowledgeable as the men and wome=
n I so deeply admired.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom=
:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Of course, Stenge=
l suffered moments of self-doubt, common among young founders. But he perse=
vered, reaching out
to his friend <strong>Kevin Ryan</strong>, the former founder of DoubleClic=
k and proprietor of a venture capital firm called AlleyCorp. They talked ab=
out his idea of making a chatbot for financial data. Ryan urged Gabe to tal=
k to his partners about the idea. They talked and <em>talked</em>. =E2=80=
=9CI was such a bad marketer back then,=E2=80=9D he said. To describe Rogo,=
 he was using the ponderous =E2=80=98<em>natural language interface for fin=
ancial econometrics</em>,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9CI didn=E2=80=
=99t quite have the spiel down.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;mar=
gin-bottom:0px;">Eventually, though, Ryan decided to make a discreet bet. H=
e told Gabe and John that he would give them $2 million at an $8 million va=
luation. =E2=80=9CMy jaw kind of dropped,=E2=80=9D Gabe told me. =E2=80=9CW=
e got in the elevator, and we were like, =E2=80=98What is going on?=E2=80=
=99 Like, =E2=80=98Is this for real?=E2=80=99 Like, =E2=80=98Did we do some=
thing wrong?=E2=80=99 Like, it felt crazy, and it also just felt like there=
 was no world in which I didn=E2=80=99t want to dive in and
do it full time.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;"=
>=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Rogo has since raised t=
wo more rounds of financing: a $7 million seed round last February, adding =
Company Ventures, BoxGroup, and ScOp Ventures as investors; and then an $18=
=2E5 million round in October, led by b=
lue-chippers <strong>Keith Rabois </st=
rong>at Khosla Ventures, <strong>Eric Schmidt</strong>, and <strong>Jack Al=
tman</strong>, brother of <strong>Sam</strong>. =E2=80=9CThe folks who
got in at the $8 million valuation are definitely pleased,=E2=80=9D he said=
, after declining to tell me the valuation upon which the Series A had been=
 secured. Rogo plans to raise more money soon, Gabe said, at a valuation po=
tentially well into the high nine figures.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-b=
ottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Rogo can do in minutes what a =
junior banker would normally do in one long, sleep-deprived week: spit out =
company profiles in a nanosecond, take financial data from a
variety of sources and put together any number of different comparative ana=
lyses, etcetera. And it can do in seconds many of the essential, but rote, =
tasks that often require teams of analysts. Needless to say, many of those =
on the lower rung of the investment banking pyramid are pining for the tool=
, if for no other reason than to be more efficient. =E2=80=9CWe got a piece=
 of feedback yesterday from a junior analyst at a public independent bank w=
ho said, =E2=80=98This saved my life this week. It saved me 100
hours. And I had to borrow my friend=E2=80=99s account, because they haven=
=E2=80=99t rolled it out to everyone in my group yet.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Gab=
e told me. =E2=80=9CThat=E2=80=99s what I love to hear.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><=
/div>

a yellow sheet of paper and a pencil.=E2=80=9D Obviously, those days are lo=

<table class=3D"c4 np" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellsp=

ng-bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:&#39=
;Inter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0=
px;line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">As best as I can figure, firms such as Gol=
dman, JPMorgan Chase,
Guggenheim Partners, Moelis & Co., Wells Fargo, Apollo, GTCR, Tiger Glo=
bal, and Lazard are <em>all</em> experimenting with A.I. to do many of the =
tasks (in minutes) that used to be done by individuals over a much longer p=
eriod of time. The idea is to make work on Wall Street more efficient so ba=
nkers have more time to do the things that matter most=E2=80=94like provide=
 high-quality advice to clients. (Of course, the A.I. output would have to =
be checked thoroughly, just as an analyst=E2=80=99s work
would.)=C2=A0=C2=A0
</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0=
;margin-bottom:0px;">Naturally, one wonders whether rapid advances in A.I. =
capabilities will improve the work-life balance of junior bankers=E2=80=94a=
lleviating the multiyear hazing period that deters so many bright young peo=
ple from Wall Street=E2=80=94or whether they=E2=80=99ll reduce the opportun=
ities that constitute the finance career pipeline. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re r=
eplacing work, we=E2=80=99re not replacing people,=E2=80=9D Gabe recently
<a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2Uktx=
clJLZ0dVcFBOMW1KVS1yWW8xMWJXQjNjOD0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cueW91dHViZS5=
jb20vd2F0Y2g_dj1PUEcyYXJNSDdRd1x1MDAyNmFiX2NoYW5uZWw9VGhlUHJvZkdQb2QlRTIlOD=
AlOTNTY290dEdhbGxvd2F5XHUwMDI2dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPURyeStQb3dkZXIrLStTVUJTQ1JJQ=
kVSUyslMjgxJTJGMjYlMkYyNSUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PURyeStQb3dkZXIrLStTVUJT=
Q1JJQkVSUyslMjgxJTJGMjYlMkYyNSUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0aW9uXHU=
wMDI2dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1jdXN0b21lci5pb1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZD=
EyYSIsImludGVybmFsIjoiZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6MjA1OTIxNX0/c=
d71655c67c224ebc609bcfc2dfe3ad5a41a15725fb0ca3eb4f20deb57762a39" style=3D"t=
ext-decoration:underline;color:#d40000;">argued</a> on <strong>Scott Gallow=
ay</strong>=E2=80=99s podcast. =E2=80=9CEven five years ago, before Covid, =
you probably had investment banking analysts=E2=80=94after staying up all n=
ight creating a pitch deck, they would go print it out, bind the books, del=
iver the books to their managing directors=E2=80=99 and partners=E2=80=99 a=
partments. That would take a few hours. No one=E2=80=99s doing
that anymore, because everything=E2=80=99s virtual. It=E2=80=99s not like t=
hese bankers are working less, right? They=E2=80=99re just filling up their=
 time doing smarter, more interesting things.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margi=
n:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=
Gabe doesn=E2=80=99t expect Rogo to be able to provide the wisdom and bespo=
ke advice that, say, <strong>Blair Effron</strong> or Peter Orszag can give=
 the C.E.O. clients of Centerview Partners or Lazard. =E2=80=9CBut,=E2=80=
=9D he said, =E2=80=9CI think everyone is
underrating how smart these tools are going to get, and underrating how cre=
ative they=E2=80=99re going to be and everything else about them.=E2=80=9D =
He continued: =E2=80=9CPeople have not really thought about what that means=
 for Wall Street, what that means for these institutions. It=E2=80=99s goin=
g to transform what a bank looks like, what a hedge fund looks like, what B=
lackstone looks like five or 10 years from now.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;=
">Gabe has made a
believer out of Orszag, who, at the beginning of the month, added the title=
 of chairman of the Lazard board of directors to his C.E.O. title. (Well do=
ne, Peter.) Lazard is using a bespoke version of Rogo, which sits inside it=
s firewall, across its investment banking division, and is getting ready to=
 roll Rogo out for its asset management business; it also uses a version of=
 ChatGPT, called Lazard GPT. Orszag is quite fond of another A.I. tool as w=
ell: NotebookLM, a Google product, which lets you
turn a written document into a podcast so you can listen to an analyst=E2=
=80=99s report, for instance, rather than read it.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;=
">Orszag told me that the idea he=E2=80=99d had for Gabe at Lazard was clea=
rly ahead of its time, but that it=E2=80=99s now a banal reality. =E2=80=9C=
The history of business is littered with enterprises that had a very succes=
sful operating model and a very successful business, but didn=E2=80=99t con=
tinue to innovate and
didn=E2=80=99t see the transformational trends in time, and then saw a rapi=
d change in their circumstances,=E2=80=9D Orszag said. =E2=80=9COne of the =
reasons why I think Lazard has been around for 176 years is our ability to =
continually evolve and adapt.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin=
-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Peter agrees with many leade=
rs in the industry that A.I. won=E2=80=99t replace bankers (<em>quelle horr=
eur</em>), but rather the bankers who have access to A.I. and aren=E2=80=99=
t using it. He said tools
such as Rogo and Lazard GPT will help Lazard management develop internal ta=
lent more quickly, as employees leverage the information these tools provid=
e and use them to develop the all-important skills a senior banker must hav=
e: the ability to cultivate clients, develop relationships, and provide C.E=
=2EO.s with valuable deal insights.=20=
=E2=80=9CPersonal, trusted relationships=20=
will give you insights that no tool is going to have,=E2=80=9D Orszag said.=
 =E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t know exactly how the future will evolve, but I=E2=
=80=99ve spent
some time thinking through what the possible consequences could be. And I t=
hink, if anything, those personal connections are going to be even more imp=
ortant if some of what a banker does gets commoditized, for lack of better =
phrasing.=E2=80=9D In the meantime, he said, =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re all in =
on A.I.=E2=80=9D</p></div>
